enrichment.short_description: Digital analytics platform that autocaptures every user interaction so teams can retroactively analyze behavior without pre-instrumenting events.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans (Growth/Pro/Premier) start around $3,600/year with custom quotes

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Heap is a digital analytics and product analytics platform built around a core idea: instead of asking teams to decide in advance which clicks, taps, and form submissions matter, it captures everything automatically the moment a tracking snippet is installed. This 'autocapture' model means product managers, analysts, and growth teams can go back later and define new events on historical data, rather than waiting weeks for engineering to add tracking and only then starting to collect data.

Founded in 2013 in Silicon Valley by a group of Stanford computer science graduates through Y Combinator, Heap grew into one of the more prominent independent product analytics companies before being acquired by Contentsquare in December 2023. Since the acquisition, Heap has been positioned alongside Contentsquare's experience analytics and session replay tools as part of a combined product and digital experience analytics suite, while continuing to operate and sell as the Heap product.

At its core, Heap answers questions like where users drop off in a funnel, which features drive retention, and how specific segments of users behave differently, all without requiring a data or engineering team to hand-write every tracking call. It's aimed at product, growth, and marketing teams at SaaS and web/mobile app companies who need self-serve behavioral analytics without a heavy up-front instrumentation project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Heap Is For
paragraphs: Heap is best suited to product managers, growth marketers, and UX researchers at SaaS companies, e-commerce platforms, and consumer or B2B web/mobile applications who need to understand user behavior without depending entirely on engineering resources. Because autocapture collects interaction data by default, non-technical team members can define and analyze new events after the fact using a point-and-click interface rather than filing a ticket for new tracking code.

It also fits organizations that are still discovering which metrics matter. Early-stage startups exploring product-market fit benefit from being able to ask new behavioral questions of historical data as their understanding of the product evolves, since the data was already being captured before anyone knew exactly what to look for. Growth teams running frequent onboarding or funnel experiments can similarly iterate on event definitions without redeploying tracking code each time.

Larger organizations that need account-level analytics for B2B products, session replay tied directly to funnel drop-off points, or integration with a data warehouse also fit well into Heap's higher tiers, which add engagement scoring, behavioral targeting, and dedicated customer success support for teams running analytics at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Teams with very tight data volume or budget constraints may find Heap's session-based pricing model becomes expensive quickly once they outgrow the free tier, since paid tiers are quoted individually based on monthly session volume rather than published at a flat rate. Organizations that need firm, transparent list pricing before evaluating a tool may be frustrated by having to go through a sales conversation to get a quote for Growth, Pro, or Premier plans.

Companies that already rely heavily on a fully event-based, manually instrumented analytics pipeline (for example, teams deeply invested in a warehouse-first or dbt-driven analytics stack with precise, hand-defined events) may not see as much incremental value from autocapture, since much of Heap's differentiation comes from reducing instrumentation work that those teams have already built out.

Very small teams or solo builders with minimal traffic and simple analytics needs may also find Heap's feature set more than they need; lighter-weight or free tools focused purely on pageviews and basic conversion tracking can be a simpler fit until the product and its questions grow more complex.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Heap's foundational feature is autocapture: once the tracking snippet is installed on a web or mobile app, it automatically records clicks, pageviews, form interactions, and taps without requiring developers to manually define events in code. Paired with this is retroactive analysis, which lets analysts create new event definitions on top of already-collected historical data through a visual labeling interface, avoiding the common analytics problem of realizing too late that a key interaction was never tracked.

On top of the captured data, Heap provides standard product analytics building blocks: funnels, user journeys, retention charts, and dashboards for tracking product usage over time. Higher tiers add session replay that links directly to specific analytics events so teams can watch exactly what a user did around a friction point, along with account-level analytics and engagement scoring for B2B products where usage needs to be understood at the company level rather than just the individual user level.

Additional platform capabilities include Heap Connect for syncing data to a data warehouse, Heap Activate for pushing behavioral segments into other marketing and product tools, and an AI assistant (marketed as Sense) that helps surface insights or answer questions about the underlying data. The platform also supports SSO and standard compliance certifications like GDPR, CCPA, and SOC 2, which matter for enterprise buyers evaluating it alongside other analytics vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Heap Experience
paragraphs: Getting started with Heap typically involves adding a JavaScript snippet (or mobile SDK) to a site or app, after which data begins flowing in automatically without any further setup. New users often start in the Free plan, exploring pre-built charts and defining their first custom events retroactively once they see what interactions were already captured, which shortens the time between installation and getting a usable answer to a specific question.

As teams move into paid tiers, the day-to-day experience shifts toward building and sharing dashboards, setting up funnels for key conversion paths, and using session replay to investigate specific drop-off points flagged by the analytics. The point-and-click event definition workflow remains central throughout, letting non-technical users adjust what's being measured without waiting on engineering, though very complex custom logic can still require some collaboration with a data or dev team.

Since the Contentsquare acquisition, some customers have experienced product changes as the two platforms' capabilities (digital experience analytics and product analytics) are gradually integrated, so prospective buyers evaluating Heap should confirm current roadmap and support details directly with the vendor rather than relying solely on pre-acquisition materials.

features.title: Autocapture
features.description: Automatically records clicks, pageviews, form submissions, and taps across web and mobile as soon as the tracking snippet is installed, with no manual event instrumentation required.

features.title: Retroactive event definition
features.description: Lets analysts define new events on top of already-captured historical data through a visual, point-and-click interface, so questions that weren't anticipated up front can still be answered.

features.title: Funnels, journeys, and retention analysis
features.description: Standard product analytics building blocks for visualizing where users convert or drop off, the paths they take through a product, and how well cohorts retain over time.

features.title: Session replay
features.description: Watch recordings of individual user sessions cued to specific analytics events, helping teams see exactly what happened around a friction point or drop-off.

features.title: Account analytics and engagement matrix
features.description: Aggregates individual user behavior up to the account level for B2B products, useful for scoring engagement and prioritizing accounts at risk of churn.

features.title: Heap Connect
features.description: Syncs captured event data into a data warehouse so it can be combined with other business data for custom modeling or BI reporting.

features.title: Heap Activate
features.description: Pushes behavioral segments and audiences built in Heap out to other marketing, product, or messaging tools for targeting.

features.title: Sense AI assistant
features.description: An AI-assisted layer for surfacing insights and helping users interpret analytics data without manually building every chart from scratch.

features.title: Chart customization and dashboards
features.description: Configurable charts and shareable dashboards for tracking key product metrics across teams.

features.title: SSO and compliance certifications
features.description: Enterprise-friendly single sign-on support along with GDPR, CCPA, and SOC 2 compliance for organizations with strict data governance requirements.

pros: Autocapture removes the need to pre-plan and hand-instrument every event before collecting data
Retroactive analysis lets teams answer new questions using data collected before the question was even asked
Point-and-click event definition makes ad hoc analysis accessible to non-technical team members
Session replay tied to specific analytics events helps teams quickly investigate friction points
Free tier lets small teams or early-stage products start collecting data without upfront cost
cons: Paid plan pricing is not publicly listed and requires a custom sales quote
Session-based pricing can scale up quickly as traffic grows
Advanced features like session replay and account analytics are gated behind higher tiers
Autocaptured raw data can require cleanup or filtering to avoid noisy, low-value events
Post-acquisition integration with Contentsquare has introduced some product and roadmap uncertainty

faqs.question: What does 'autocapture' mean in Heap?
faqs.answer: Autocapture means Heap automatically records user interactions such as clicks, pageviews, and form submissions as soon as its tracking snippet is installed, without requiring developers to manually define each event beforehand.

faqs.question: Can Heap analyze data from before an event was defined?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because autocapture collects interaction data by default, Heap allows retroactive analysis, letting users define new events on historical data that was already captured, rather than only tracking going forward.

faqs.question: Is Heap free to use?
faqs.answer: Heap offers a free plan covering up to a set number of monthly sessions with core analytics features and limited data history. Paid Growth, Pro, and Premier tiers unlock higher session volumes, longer data retention, and advanced features.

faqs.question: How much do Heap's paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans are priced based on monthly session volume and are not listed publicly; pricing for Growth, Pro, and Premier tiers requires contacting Heap's sales team for a custom quote.

faqs.question: Is Heap still a separate product after the Contentsquare acquisition?
faqs.answer: Yes, Heap continues to operate and be sold as its own product following Contentsquare's acquisition in December 2023, while also being integrated into Contentsquare's broader experience analytics suite over time.

use_cases.title: Diagnosing funnel drop-off
use_cases.description: Use funnels built on autocaptured events to identify exactly where users abandon a signup, checkout, or onboarding flow, then use session replay to see what happened at that step.

use_cases.title: Retroactive feature adoption analysis
use_cases.description: Define new events after a feature has already launched to measure adoption and engagement using data collected since the feature went live, without needing to re-instrument tracking.

use_cases.title: Account-level engagement scoring for B2B SaaS
use_cases.description: Roll individual user activity up to the account level to identify engaged versus at-risk accounts, supporting customer success and expansion efforts.
use_cases.audience: Customer success and B2B SaaS teams

use_cases.title: Early-stage product-market fit exploration
use_cases.description: Capture all user behavior from day one so an early-stage team can explore which actions correlate with retention or conversion before committing to a fixed analytics schema.
use_cases.audience: Startup founders and early product teams

features: Unlimited projects and advanced user permissions

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Heap is a digital analytics platform that autocaptures every user click, tap, and form event so teams can retroactively analyze behavior without pre-planning.

enrichment.short_description: Height was an AI-driven project management and issue-tracking tool for product teams; the service was discontinued in September 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Height was a project management and issue-tracking platform built for product and engineering teams, founded in 2018 by Michael Villar, who had previously co-founded Kickoff, a productivity company acquired by Stripe in 2013. Headquartered in New York, Height raised roughly $18.3 million in backing led by Redpoint Ventures and spent several years positioning itself as a faster, more design-forward alternative to Jira and Asana before pivoting toward autonomous, AI-driven workflows.

The product combined flexible task lists, custom attributes, and multiple views (Kanban boards, tables, timelines, and calendars) with a reasoning engine the team called Autopilot. Autopilot could triage incoming bug reports, update task statuses from linked Slack conversations, draft specs, and flag duplicate tickets, aiming to remove much of the manual bookkeeping that usually falls on engineering managers and product owners.

Height announced in March 2025 that it would shut down, and the service stopped operating on September 24, 2025. The company did not publicly detail a single cause, though community discussion pointed to intensifying competition from far better-funded rivals such as Linear, ClickUp, Asana, and Jira. This profile is retained for historical and evaluative reference; the product is no longer available to new or existing customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Height Is For
paragraphs: When it was operating, Height was best suited to small and mid-sized product and engineering teams that wanted a fast, keyboard-driven alternative to heavier tools like Jira. Its free tier, which allowed unlimited members and tasks, made it attractive to early-stage startups that needed real project tracking without committing to a per-seat budget from day one.

Teams that valued automation over manual process also gravitated toward Height. Engineering managers who were tired of manually triaging bug reports or updating ticket statuses after every Slack thread found real value in Autopilot's ability to handle that legwork, and design-conscious teams appreciated the polished, minimal interface over the denser, form-heavy screens common in older enterprise trackers.

Because Height integrated with GitHub, GitLab, Figma, Sentry, and Slack, it particularly suited software teams whose workflows already lived across those tools and who wanted a single tracker to sit at the center of that stack rather than a generic to-do list. Remote-first teams also liked that Autopilot could keep a shared spec up to date automatically, reducing the coordination overhead that distributed teams usually feel most acutely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Anyone evaluating project management software today should avoid relying on Height, since the company shut down operations on September 24, 2025, stopped accepting new signups, and is no longer accessible to existing customers. Teams researching this profile for historical context should look at active alternatives such as Linear, ClickUp, Asana, or Jira instead.

Large enterprises that need long-term vendor stability, dedicated account management, and guaranteed longevity were poorly served even while Height was live, since it was a small, venture-backed startup without the scale of its bigger competitors. Its eventual shutdown underscores that risk for any team choosing a niche, venture-funded tool over an established incumbent for mission-critical workflows.

Organizations that depend on a deep marketplace of third-party plugins and integrations would also have found Height limiting; its integration list, while solid for engineering workflows, was materially smaller than what Jira, Asana, or ClickUp offer through their app ecosystems. Compliance-heavy industries requiring extensive audit trails or dedicated data-residency controls were similarly a poor fit for a lean startup product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Height's signature feature was Autopilot, an embedded AI reasoning engine that watched task activity and linked conversations to automate routine project management chores. It could auto-triage incoming bug reports, apply labels and priority levels based on ticket content, detect likely duplicate issues, and keep a running product specification synchronized with decisions made in linked Slack threads.

Beyond automation, Height offered the core building blocks expected of a modern tracker: customizable Lists with configurable attributes, multiple visualization modes including Kanban boards, tables, and timelines, and granular filtering and grouping so teams could build views tailored to sprints, roadmaps, or bug queues. Custom fields let teams model their own workflow states rather than being locked into a fixed schema.

Collaboration and integration rounded out the feature set. Height connected with Slack, GitHub, GitLab, Figma, Sentry, Notion, and Google Sheets, letting engineering activity and design handoffs flow directly into tasks. Native desktop apps for macOS and Windows, plus companion mobile apps for iOS and Android, meant the tracker could follow a team across devices rather than living only in a browser tab, and an automation rules engine let teams script their own conditional actions on top of what Autopilot already handled.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Height Experience
paragraphs: Reviewers consistently praised Height's interface for its speed and keyboard-first design, which felt closer to a code editor than a typical project management tool. Actions like creating a task, switching views, or filtering a list could be done almost entirely without touching a mouse, a detail that resonated with the engineering-heavy audience the product targeted.

The onboarding experience emphasized flexibility: new workspaces started with a blank canvas of Lists that teams could shape to their own process, rather than forcing a rigid methodology. This won Height a loyal following among startups and small teams who found heavier competitors like Jira cumbersome to configure and slow to use day to day.

That same small-team ethos, however, meant Height never achieved the scale of its rivals, and its final months were marked by a quieter cadence of product updates and social activity. The shutdown announcement in March 2025 came as a surprise to much of its user base, closing the chapter on a product that had built a genuinely devoted, if comparatively small, community around its design and automation ambitions.

features.title: Autopilot AI engine
features.description: An embedded reasoning engine that auto-triaged bug reports, applied labels and priority levels, and detected likely duplicate tasks.

features.title: Live product specs
features.description: Autopilot monitored linked Slack conversations for decisions and open questions and kept a running project specification synchronized automatically.

features.title: Flexible Lists
features.description: Tasks lived in customizable Lists with configurable attributes, letting teams model their own workflow rather than a fixed schema.

features.title: Multiple views
features.description: Kanban boards, tables, timelines, and calendar views let teams visualize the same underlying data in the format that fit each workflow.

features.title: Custom attributes and fields
features.description: Teams could define their own fields for priority, status, effort, or any other dimension relevant to their process.

features.title: Slack integration
features.description: Two-way sync between Slack conversations and Height tasks kept discussions and ticket status aligned without manual updates.

features.title: GitHub and GitLab integration
features.description: Commits, branches, and pull requests could be linked directly to tasks, connecting code activity to project tracking.

features.title: Design and monitoring integrations
features.description: Connections to Figma and Sentry brought design handoffs and error reports directly into the task tracker.

features.title: Automation rules engine
features.description: Teams could script conditional automations on top of Autopilot to fit workflows the AI didn't already cover.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native desktop apps for macOS and Windows plus companion mobile apps for iOS and Android kept the tracker accessible across devices.

pros: Fast, keyboard-first interface that felt closer to a code editor than a typical tracker
Autopilot AI reduced manual bug triage, status updates, and backlog grooming
Generous free plan with unlimited members and tasks for small teams
Deep integrations with developer tools like GitHub, GitLab, Figma, and Sentry
Flexible custom Lists and multiple views instead of a single rigid workflow
cons: The service was shut down on September 24, 2025, and is no longer available to any customers
Smaller integration marketplace than larger competitors like Jira, Asana, or ClickUp
Automation rules and Autopilot configuration took time to learn for teams new to the tool
As a small, venture-backed startup it lacked the enterprise support and longevity of bigger rivals
Fewer third-party apps and plugins than established project management platforms

faqs.question: Is Height still available to use?
faqs.answer: No. Height announced its shutdown in March 2025 and the service stopped operating on September 24, 2025. It no longer accepts new signups and existing customer data is no longer accessible.

faqs.question: What was Height's Autopilot feature?
faqs.answer: Autopilot was Height's embedded AI reasoning engine that auto-triaged bug reports, updated task statuses from linked Slack conversations, drafted specs, and flagged duplicate tickets.

faqs.question: Who founded Height and when?
faqs.answer: Height was founded in 2018 by Michael Villar, who had previously co-founded Kickoff, a productivity company acquired by Stripe in 2013.

faqs.question: What are good alternatives to Height?
faqs.answer: Teams that used Height have commonly migrated to Linear, ClickUp, Asana, or Jira, depending on whether they prioritized a fast engineering-focused interface or broader enterprise features.

faqs.question: Did Height have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, while it was operating Height offered a free plan with unlimited members and tasks, alongside paid Team, Business, and Enterprise tiers with additional automation and administrative features.

use_cases.title: Automated bug triage
use_cases.description: Engineering teams routed incoming bug reports through Autopilot, which applied labels, priority levels, and flagged likely duplicates automatically.

use_cases.title: Living product specifications
use_cases.description: Product managers linked Slack discussion threads to tasks so Autopilot could keep a shared spec document updated with decisions as they were made.

use_cases.title: Cross-functional roadmap planning
use_cases.description: Teams used custom Lists, attributes, and timeline views to plan and track roadmaps spanning design, engineering, and go-to-market work.

use_cases.title: Startup backlog management
use_cases.description: Early-stage startups used the free plan's unlimited members and tasks to run lightweight sprint planning and backlog grooming without per-seat costs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Height was an AI-powered project management and issue-tracking tool with an autopilot engine for product and engineering teams. Discontinued September 2025.

enrichment.short_description: Heroku is a cloud platform-as-a-service owned by Salesforce that lets developers deploy, manage, and scale apps via git push, dynos, and buildpacks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Heroku is a cloud platform-as-a-service (PaaS) founded in 2007 and acquired by Salesforce in 2010, built to let developers deploy and run web applications without managing servers, operating systems, or networking configuration directly. It abstracts infrastructure into a small set of primitives, most notably the dyno, a lightweight container that runs application processes and scales horizontally on demand.

The platform's signature workflow is deploying code with a simple git push, which triggers Heroku's buildpack system to detect the application's language, install dependencies, and produce a runnable slug automatically. This removes most of the manual provisioning and configuration work associated with traditional hosting or self-managed cloud infrastructure.

Around this core, Heroku has built an ecosystem of managed data services and third-party add-ons, covering databases, caching, logging, monitoring, and messaging, that can be attached to an app with a single command. Since discontinuing its long-standing free dyno tier in November 2022, Heroku now operates purely as a paid, usage-billed platform aimed at teams that value speed and simplicity over granular infrastructure control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Heroku Is For
paragraphs: Heroku suits developers and small teams who want to ship a working web application or API quickly and don't want to spend engineering time on server provisioning, container orchestration, or networking setup. Solo founders and early-stage startups building an MVP often choose it specifically to avoid standing up their own DevOps practice before they have product-market fit.

It also fits agencies and consultancies delivering client projects in common web languages like Ruby, Node.js, Python, Java, or PHP, where predictable git-based deployment and a managed add-on catalog reduce the operational overhead of running many small client apps in parallel.

Because it's a Salesforce product, Heroku is a natural fit for organizations already invested in the Salesforce ecosystem, particularly through Heroku Connect, which synchronizes data between a Heroku Postgres database and Salesforce, making it appealing for teams building custom applications that need to stay in sync with CRM data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Teams that are highly cost-sensitive at scale, or that already have infrastructure expertise in-house, will typically find raw cloud providers like AWS, Google Cloud, or DigitalOcean cheaper once traffic and resource needs grow, since Heroku's dyno and add-on pricing carries a convenience premium over comparable raw compute and managed database pricing.

Organizations that need deep infrastructure customization, such as custom networking topologies, specialized Kubernetes configurations, GPU workloads, or fine-grained control over underlying compute instances, will find Heroku's abstraction layer restrictive rather than helpful, since it deliberately limits what can be configured.

Hobbyists and students looking for a permanently free way to host small personal projects should also look elsewhere, since Heroku removed its free dyno and free database tiers in 2022; every plan today, including the low-cost Eco tier, requires a paid subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Heroku's core building blocks are dynos and buildpacks: dynos are isolated containers that run web, worker, or scheduled processes, while buildpacks automatically detect an app's language and dependencies to build a deployable slug, supporting Ruby, Node.js, Python, Java, PHP, Go, Scala, and Clojure out of the box, with custom buildpacks available for other stacks.

The Heroku Add-ons marketplace lets teams attach managed services, such as Heroku Postgres, Heroku Redis, logging tools, monitoring dashboards, and email or search providers, to an app with a single CLI command, avoiding the need to separately provision and maintain these services.

Heroku Pipelines, review apps, and GitHub integration support a structured CI/CD workflow, automatically spinning up temporary staging environments for pull requests and promoting builds through development, staging, and production stages, while the Heroku CLI and dashboard give developers command-line and visual control over scaling, config variables, logs, and releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Heroku Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Heroku feels centered on the terminal: developers configure environment variables, scale dynos, and tail logs through the Heroku CLI, while the web dashboard offers a visual layer for managing add-ons, collaborators, and pipeline stages across multiple apps.

The platform's biggest strength is how little ceremony stands between writing code and having it running in production; a working app can typically be live within minutes of a first deploy, and add-ons remove much of the friction of wiring up a database or logging service.

The trade-off many teams eventually feel is cost and flexibility as usage grows: dyno and add-on pricing scales in fairly large steps rather than smoothly, and once workloads become large or specialized enough, teams often start evaluating whether the operational simplicity Heroku provides still outweighs the price premium over managing infrastructure directly on a hyperscale cloud provider.

features.title: Dynos
features.description: Lightweight, isolated Linux containers that run your application's web, worker, or one-off processes and scale horizontally on demand.

features.title: Buildpacks
features.description: Automatic detection and build scripts for languages like Ruby, Node.js, Python, Java, PHP, and Go that turn source code into a deployable slug.

features.title: Git-based deployment
features.description: Deploy new releases with a simple git push to Heroku, which triggers an automatic build and release without manual server configuration.

features.title: Add-ons marketplace
features.description: A catalog of managed third-party services, including databases, caching, logging, and monitoring, that attach to an app with one command.

features.title: Heroku Postgres
features.description: A managed PostgreSQL database service with automated backups, follower databases, and monitoring built directly into the platform.

features.title: Pipelines and review apps
features.description: Structured CI/CD workflows that promote code through development, staging, and production while spinning up temporary review environments for pull requests.

features.title: Heroku CLI
features.description: A command-line tool for managing apps, config variables, dynos, logs, and add-ons directly from the terminal.

features.title: Autoscaling and metrics
features.description: Built-in application metrics and autoscaling rules on higher-tier dynos to help maintain performance under variable traffic.

features.title: Heroku Connect
features.description: A synchronization service that keeps data flowing between a Heroku app's database and Salesforce, useful for teams already on the Salesforce platform.

pros: Extremely fast, low-friction deployment workflow built around a simple git push
Wide native language support through buildpacks, plus custom buildpacks for others
Large add-on marketplace that removes manual setup for databases, caching, and monitoring
Mature CI/CD tooling with pipelines, review apps, and GitHub integration
Backed by Salesforce, giving it long-term operational stability and Salesforce ecosystem integration
cons: No permanent free tier since the 2022 removal of free dynos and databases
Costs can rise quickly compared to raw cloud infrastructure as apps scale
Less granular control over underlying infrastructure than platforms like AWS or GCP
Add-on pricing is layered on top of dyno costs, which can make total cost less predictable
Limited support for specialized workloads such as GPU-heavy or highly custom networking setups

faqs.question: Does Heroku still offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: No. Heroku discontinued its free dyno and free database tiers in November 2022; the lowest-cost option today is the paid Eco plan.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Heroku support?
faqs.answer: Heroku natively supports Ruby, Node.js, Python, Java, PHP, Go, Scala, and Clojure through official buildpacks, and custom buildpacks can extend support to other languages.

faqs.question: Who owns Heroku?
faqs.answer: Heroku has been a wholly owned subsidiary of Salesforce since Salesforce completed its acquisition of the company in early 2011.

faqs.question: How does deployment work on Heroku?
faqs.answer: Developers typically deploy by pushing code to a Heroku-managed git repository, which triggers an automatic build via buildpacks and releases the app to running dynos.

faqs.question: Is Heroku a good fit for large-scale enterprise applications?
faqs.answer: It can be, especially for teams that value operational simplicity, but very large or highly specialized workloads often become more cost-effective on a raw cloud provider like AWS or Google Cloud.

use_cases.title: Launching an MVP quickly
use_cases.description: Founders and small teams deploy a working web app or API in minutes without provisioning servers or configuring infrastructure themselves.

use_cases.title: Client project delivery
use_cases.description: Agencies host multiple client applications with predictable git-based deployment and managed add-ons instead of building custom infrastructure for each project.

use_cases.title: Salesforce-connected applications
use_cases.description: Teams build custom apps on Heroku that sync data with Salesforce in near real time using Heroku Connect.

use_cases.title: Internal tools and APIs
use_cases.description: Engineering teams stand up internal dashboards, APIs, and microservices without dedicating DevOps resources to manage them.

features: 1,000 shared compute hours per month across Eco dynos
Dynos sleep after inactivity to conserve hours
Good for low-traffic apps and side projects
Access to the Heroku add-ons marketplace
Dedicated dyno that does not sleep
Simple pricing per dyno per month
Suitable for small production or staging apps
512 MB to 1 GB RAM per dyno
Improved performance isolation for high-traffic apps

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Heroku is Salesforce's cloud PaaS for deploying and scaling web apps via git push, dynos, buildpacks, and a managed add-ons marketplace.
